title: Delegation & Parallel Work
summary: Practical patterns for using Hermes subagent delegation effectively: parallel research, code review in isolated context, comparing alternatives, multi-file refactoring, gather-then-analyze split, toolset selection, and concurrency/depth configuration.

config_keys.description: Parallel batch size per delegate_task call
How many delegation levels can spawn further (1-3)
Allow orchestrator subagents to spawn further subagents

code_examples.caption: Parallel research
Security code review
code_examples.code:
delegate_task(
    goal="Review src/auth/ for security issues and fix any found",
    context="""Project at /home/user/webapp. Python 3.11, Flask, PyJWT, bcrypt.
    Auth files: src/auth/login.py, src/auth/jwt.py, src/auth/middleware.py
    Test command: pytest tests/auth/ -v
    Focus on: SQL injection, JWT validation, password hashing, session management.
    Fix issues found and verify tests pass.""",
    toolsets=["terminal", "file"]
)

gotchas: Subagents know NOTHING about your conversation — pass file paths, error messages, project structure, and constraints explicitly in context
delegate_task is synchronous — if the parent turn is interrupted, all active children are cancelled and work is discarded
For work that must outlive the current turn, use cronjob or terminal(background=True, notify_on_complete=True)
Leaf subagents cannot call delegate_task, clarify, memory, send_message, or execute_code by default
Parallel subagents editing the SAME file can conflict — handle shared files yourself after parallel work completes
Default 50 iterations per subagent — lower max_iterations for simple tasks to save cost
full_text:
# Delegation & Parallel Work

Hermes can spawn isolated child agents to work on tasks in parallel. Each subagent gets its own conversation, terminal session, and toolset.
